message: Most buttons have helpful usage tips if you hover over them
PsychoPy can handle many different units (degrees of visual angle, cm...) for your stimuli. But you need to tell it about your monitor first (see the online documentation on General > Units)
Menu items show you the current key bindings (which can be configured in preferences)
It's a really good idea to run in pilot mode and check your data are all being saved as you like BEFORE running all of your participants!
You can control what is different in pilot mode (compared than run mode) in the 'Piloting' section of Preferences
The [user forum](https://discourse.psychopy.org) is a great place to ask questions and receive updates
If you write to the forum, make it clear whether you're running locally or online.
You can use the source. PsychoPy comes with all its source code included. If you know Python, you'd be surprised how easy it is to find your own bug-fixes.
Degrees of visual angle are 'device independent'. Normalized units mean that your stimulus will be different on each different computer. Do you really want that?
PsychoPy is free. Please [cite the most recent paper](https://psychopy.org/about/index.html#citing-psychopy) if you use PsychoPy in published work
In Python, the values True and False must have capitals and really just stand for 1 and 0.
Did your stimulus not appear? Was it really tiny? Is it the same color as your background?
The default color values in PsychoPy range from -1 to +1, with 0 being the mean grey of the screen. So black is like the maximum decrement from grey and white is an increment. Right?

Data can be output in many different formats, but it's worth saving the 'psydat' (aka pickle) format as well as the others. Although this isn't 'human readable' it stores more information than excel/csv files including an entire copy of your actual experiment!
You can see how many people used PsychoPy this month at [usage.psychopy.org](https://usage.psychopy.org)
If you like PsychoPy spread the word
For the best audio timing you need to use the 'PTB' backend (set this in the app Preferences).
Get your department to buy a site licence for Pavlovia.org. You'll be helping fund PsychoPy development and you'll be able to run unlimited online studies.
Use informative variable and Component names. You don't want to be trawling through your experiment looking for 'text_32'...
Friend of the show Wakefield has been posting daily tips on the forum. [Give them a look!](https://discourse.psychopy.org/t/wakefields-daily-tips-index/43722)
If you're running in a browser and can't find an error message, try [opening your browser's developer tools](https://balsamiq.com/support/faqs/browser-console); it may be in the console.
Open Science Tools (that's us, the makers of PsychoPy!) offer [consultancy](https://psychopy.org/consultancy); we can teach you to use PsychoPy and even help you build an experiment
Check out [demo experiments on Pavlovia](https://pavlovia.org/explore/demos?sort=DEFAULT) for a useful starting point

It's entirely possible that someone has made an experiment similar to what you want to do, so its worth [browsing public projects](https://pavlovia.org/explore?sort=DEFAULT) on Pavlovia before you start
Stay in the loop on upcoming training events via [our workshops site](https://workshops.psychopy.org/)

Sites like [itch.io](https://itch.io/) and [Pexels](https://www.pexels.com/) can be a great resource for stimuli, especially if you want to gamify your experiment, but you should always check the license and be sure to credit the authors in your README file.
Chocolate probably won't help, but does that mean you shouldn't try it?
You should avoid snorting Pepsi
You should wear sunscreen. (Where does it say these tips have to be original?!)
Debugging is like a murder mystery, except you're the murderer...
Most dialogs have a help button that takes you to the relevant online documentation

From Builder you can use 'Compile' to generate the Python script that controls your experiment, and view or edit it in the Coder. Any edits are not reflected back in the Builder, however. [The answer is (almost) never to edit the generated code](https://discourse.psychopy.org/t/wakefields-daily-tips/43371/17).
Need sub-millisecond timing? Use Validator Routines to make sure you're *getting* sub-millisecond timing!
For fMRI block designs you want to use non-slip timing (trials of pre-determined duration). Builder indicates these with a green icon in the Flow panel
You can often right click on things to bring up a menu, e.g., to remove a trial.
To set stimulus position to your variables X and Y, you can use either $[X,Y] or [$X,Y]. (A $ anywhere indicates that the entire entry box is Python code)

The contents of the dialog box at the start of your experiment are controlled from the Experiment Settings button. You can use these values in your study by referring to the value in expInfo e.g. expInfo['participant']
If you set a description for a Routine in its Routine Settings, whatever you wrote will appear when you hover over that Routine in the flow
To put a $ symbol in a stimulus, you need to use \$, like this: 'You win \$5.00!'. (This is only for input boxes, and not for code components.)
You can use 'Ctrl + Z' and 'Ctrl + Shift + Z' to undo and redo many of the actions in the Builder, such as adding, deleting, or changing something.
In Builder you can control what appears in the 'Experiment Info' dialog box in the Experiment Settings

Sometimes the correct response on a given trial is not to respond at all, such as in a go/no-go task. To score such responses properly, enter 'None' as the correct answer (no quotes) in the Builder dialog box.

Is your experiment tedious to run? Check out the [psychopy-monkeys](https://teparsons.github.io/psychopy-monkeys/) plugin - adding a monkey to your experiment makes it easy to skip some parts in pilot mode.
Reusing Routines wherever possible will make your experiment
Did you know you can disable Routines and Components? Check the 'Testing' tab of the Component or Routine Settings
Setting 'nReps' in a loop to 0 means the whole loop is skipped
You can use a Survey Routine to embed a [Pavlovia Survey](https://pavlovia.org/docs/surveys/overview) in your experiment. Perfect for consent/debrief forms when running online!
You can comment/uncomment entire blocks of code with 'Ctrl + ?'
Comments are a love note to future you. Always comment your code!
